title: Black Box Data After a Semi Crash in Lubbock, Texas
twitter: Semi crash in Lubbock? The truck's black box data starts disappearing within DAYS. ECM & ELD records can prove what really happened — act fast. Call 855-WRECKMATCH (855-897-3256) now. https://www.wreckmatch.com/blog/black-box-data-after-a-semi-crash-in-lubbock-texas
linkedin:
If you handle personal injury or commercial trucking cases in West Texas, black box data preservation is often the difference between a strong case and a lost one.

After a semi-truck crash in Lubbock, the truck's ECM (Engine Control Module) and ELD (Electronic Logging Device) record critical data: speed, braking, hours of service, hard events, and more. Under FMCSA regulations, carriers are required to retain certain records — but that doesn't mean they will without a legal hold demand.

Here's what attorneys in our network of 800+ law firms already know: this data can vanish in as little as 30 days through routine overwriting. A spoliation letter sent immediately after a crash protects your client's right to that evidence.

WreckMatch connects injured victims in Lubbock and across Texas with experienced truck accident attorneys who know how to move fast on evidence preservation, subpoena ELD providers, and build airtight cases against commercial carriers and their insurers.

If you're an attorney looking to expand your truck accident caseload in West Texas, or a victim who needs expert legal representation after a semi crash, start here: https://www.wreckmatch.com/#form
facebook:
If someone you love was hurt in a semi-truck crash in Lubbock, you're probably dealing with a lot right now — medical bills, insurance calls, unanswered questions about what actually happened on that road.

Here's something most people don't know: every commercial semi-truck carries a black box — an ECM and often an ELD — that records speed, braking, hours driven, and more at the time of the crash. That data is critical evidence. But it doesn't last forever. In many cases, it starts overwriting itself within days or weeks.

Preserving that evidence quickly can be the key to understanding exactly what went wrong and holding the right people accountable. Whether the crash happened on US-82, the Loop, or the Marsha Sharp Freeway, the clock starts ticking the moment the crash occurs.

WreckMatch helps connect crash victims with experienced truck accident attorneys in Texas who know how to act fast and fight hard.

Have you or someone you know ever dealt with a semi-truck accident and wondered how to get answers about what really happened?
reddit_title: Semi-truck crash in Lubbock, TX — does the truck's black box data really disappear that fast?
reddit_body:
Yes, unfortunately it can. Commercial semi-trucks are equipped with an ECM (Engine Control Module) and often an ELD (Electronic Logging Device) that record things like vehicle speed, hard braking events, throttle position, hours of service, and GPS location. This data is incredibly useful in crash investigations — but ECMs often overwrite older data on a rolling basis, sometimes within 30 days or less depending on the system and how much the truck continues to be used after the crash.

The most important step after a serious semi crash is getting a spoliation letter (also called a litigation hold notice) sent to the trucking company as quickly as possible. This formally notifies them of their legal obligation to preserve all data related to the crash — including ECM data, ELD logs, driver qualification files, inspection records, and communications. Once that letter is received, destroying or allowing that data to overwrite can expose the carrier to serious legal consequences.

FMCSA regulations do require carriers to retain certain records, but enforcement depends on someone actually demanding it. If you or someone you know was involved in a semi-truck crash in Lubbock or anywhere in Texas, talking to a truck accident attorney fast matters more than most people realize. Sites like wreckmatch.com can help connect you with attorneys who specifically handle commercial trucking cases and know how to move quickly on evidence preservation.
